title: Piedmont

infobox.value: 0.912[[4]](#cite_note-GlobalDataLab-4) very high · [11th of 21](/wiki/List_of_Italian_regions_by_Human_Development_Index "List of Italian regions by Human Development Index")

([/ˈpiːdmɒnt/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*PEED-mont*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Piemonte* [[pjeˈmonte]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); [Piedmontese](/wiki/Piedmontese_language "Piedmontese language"): *Piemont* [[pjeˈmʊŋt]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Piedmontese "Help:IPA/Piedmontese"))[[a]](#cite_note-5) is one of the 20 [regions of Italy](/wiki/Regions_of_Italy "Regions of Italy"), located in the [Northwest of the country](/wiki/Northwest_Italy "Northwest Italy").[[5]](#cite_note-6) It borders the [Liguria](/wiki/Liguria "Liguria") region to the south, the [Lombardy](/wiki/Lombardy "Lombardy") and [Emilia-Romagna](/wiki/Emilia-Romagna "Emilia-Romagna") regions to the east, and the [Aosta Valley](/wiki/Aosta_Valley "Aosta Valley") region to the northwest. Piedmont also borders [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") to the north and [France](/wiki/France "France") to the west.

**Piedmont** ([/ˈpiːdmɒnt/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*PEED-mont*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Piemonte* [[pjeˈmonte]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); [Piedmontese](/wiki/Piedmontese_language "Piedmontese language"): *Piemont* [[pjeˈmʊŋt]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Piedmontese "Help:IPA/Piedmontese"))[[a]](#cite_note-5) is one of the 20 [regions of Italy](/wiki/Regions_of_Italy "Regions of Italy"), located in the [Northwest of the country](/wiki/Northwest_Italy "Northwest Italy").[[5]](#cite_note-6) It borders the [Liguria](/wiki/Liguria "Liguria") region to the south, the [Lombardy](/wiki/Lombardy "Lombardy") and [Emilia-Romagna](/wiki/Emilia-Romagna "Emilia-Romagna") regions to the east, and the [Aosta Valley](/wiki/Aosta_Valley "Aosta Valley") region to the northwest. Piedmont also borders [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") to the north and [France](/wiki/France "France") to the west.

Piedmont has an area of 25,387.07 km2 (9,802.00 mi2), making it the 2nd-largest region of Italy in area after [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily"). With a population of 4,251,868, it is also the 7th-most populous region as of 2025.[[2]](#cite_note-population-2) The capital of Piedmont is [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin"), which was the first capital of the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy"), from 1861 to 1865.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Piedmont&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The French *Piedmont*, the Italian *Piemonte*, and other variant cognates come from the [medieval Latin](/wiki/Medieval_Latin "Medieval Latin") *Pedemontium* or *Pedemontis*, i.e. *ad pedem montium*, meaning "at the foot of the mountains" (referring to the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps")), attested in documents from the end of the 12th century.[[6]](#cite_note-7)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Piedmont&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Torino-portapalatina01.jpg/250px-Torino-portapalatina01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Torino-portapalatina01.jpg)

The Roman [Palatine Towers](/wiki/Palatine_Towers "Palatine Towers") in [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin")

Piedmont was inhabited in early historic times by [Celtic](/wiki/Celts "Celts")-[Ligurian](/wiki/Ligures "Ligures") tribes such as the [Taurini](/wiki/Taurini "Taurini") and the [Salassi](/wiki/Salassi "Salassi"). They were later subdued by the [Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") (c. 220 BC), who founded several colonies there including *[Augusta Taurinorum](/wiki/Augusta_Taurinorum "Augusta Taurinorum")* (Turin) and *[Eporedia](/wiki/Eporedia "Eporedia")* ([Ivrea](/wiki/Ivrea "Ivrea")). After the fall of the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire"), the region was successively invaded by the [Burgundians](/wiki/Burgundians "Burgundians"), the [Ostrogoths](/wiki/Ostrogoths "Ostrogoths") (5th century), [East Romans](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), [Lombards](/wiki/Lombards "Lombards") (6th century), and [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks") (773).

In the 9th–10th centuries there were further incursions by the [Magyars](/wiki/Magyars "Magyars"), [Saracens](/wiki/Saracens "Saracens") and Muslim [Moors](/wiki/Moors "Moors").[[7]](#cite_note-8) At the time Piedmont, as part of the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy_(medieval) "Kingdom of Italy (medieval)") within the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"), was subdivided into several marches and counties. In 1046, [Otto of Savoy](/wiki/Otto,_Count_of_Savoy "Otto, Count of Savoy") added Piedmont to the [County of Savoy](/wiki/County_of_Savoy "County of Savoy"), with a capital at [Chambéry](/wiki/Chamb%C3%A9ry "Chambéry") (now in France). Other areas remained independent, such as the powerful *[comuni](/wiki/Comune "Comune")* (municipalities) of [Asti](/wiki/Asti "Asti") and [Alessandria](/wiki/Alessandria "Alessandria") and the marquisates of [Saluzzo](/wiki/Marquisate_of_Saluzzo "Marquisate of Saluzzo") and [Montferrat](/wiki/Marquisate_of_Montferrat "Marquisate of Montferrat"). The County of Savoy became the [Duchy of Savoy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Savoy "Duchy of Savoy") in 1416, and Duke [Emanuele Filiberto](/wiki/Emmanuel_Philibert,_Duke_of_Savoy "Emmanuel Philibert, Duke of Savoy") moved the seat to Turin in 1563. In 1720, the Duke of Savoy became King of [Sardinia](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia"), founding what evolved into the [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720%E2%80%931861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720–1861)") and increasing Turin's importance as a European capital.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Valentino_castle.jpg/250px-Valentino_castle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Valentino_castle.jpg)

[Castello del Valentino](/wiki/Castello_del_Valentino "Castello del Valentino"), one of the [House of Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy") Royal Residences

The [Republic of Alba](/wiki/Republic_of_Alba "Republic of Alba") was created in 1796 as a [French client republic](/wiki/French_client_republic "French client republic") in Piedmont. A new client republic, the [Piedmontese Republic](/wiki/Piedmontese_Republic "Piedmontese Republic"), existed between 1798 and 1799 before it was reoccupied by Austrian and Russian troops. In June 1800, a third client republic, the [Subalpine Republic](/wiki/Subalpine_Republic "Subalpine Republic"), was established in Piedmont. It fell under full French control in 1801, and France annexed it in September 1802. In the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna"), the Kingdom of Sardinia was restored and furthermore received the [Republic of Genoa](/wiki/Republic_of_Genoa "Republic of Genoa") to strengthen it as a barrier against France.

Piedmont was a springboard for [Italian unification](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification") in 1859–1861, following earlier unsuccessful wars against the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") in 1820–1821,[[8]](#cite_note-9) and 1848–1849. This process is sometimes referred to as *Piedmontisation*.[[9]](#cite_note-10) The efforts were later countered by the efforts of rural farmers.[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12) The [House of Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy") became [Kings of Italy](/wiki/Kings_of_Italy "Kings of Italy"), and Turin briefly became the capital of Italy. However, when the Italian capital was moved to [Florence](/wiki/Florence,_Italy "Florence, Italy"), and then to [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), the administrative and institutional importance of Piedmont was reduced. The only recognition of Piedmont's historical role was that the [crown prince](/wiki/Crown_prince "Crown prince") of Italy was known as the [Prince of Piedmont](/wiki/Prince_of_Piedmont "Prince of Piedmont"). After Italian unification, Piedmont was one of the most important regions in the first Italian industrialization.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Piedmont&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of Piedmont](/wiki/Geography_of_Piedmont "Geography of Piedmont")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Langhe.jpg/250px-Langhe.jpg)](/wiki/File:Langhe.jpg)

A [Langhe](/wiki/Langhe "Langhe") landscape

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Turin_monte_cappuccini.jpg/250px-Turin_monte_cappuccini.jpg)](/wiki/File:Turin_monte_cappuccini.jpg)

View of [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin")

Piedmont is surrounded on three sides by the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps"), including [Monviso](/wiki/Monte_Viso "Monte Viso"), where the [river Po](/wiki/Po_(river) "Po (river)") rises, and [Monte Rosa](/wiki/Monte_Rosa "Monte Rosa"). It borders France ([Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes](/wiki/Auvergne-Rh%C3%B4ne-Alpes "Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes") and [Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur](/wiki/Provence-Alpes-C%C3%B4te_d%27Azur "Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur")), Switzerland ([Ticino](/wiki/Ticino "Ticino") and [Valais](/wiki/Valais "Valais")), and the Italian regions of [Lombardy](/wiki/Lombardy "Lombardy"), [Liguria](/wiki/Liguria "Liguria"), [Aosta Valley](/wiki/Aosta_Valley "Aosta Valley"), and for a very small part with [Emilia Romagna](/wiki/Emilia_Romagna "Emilia Romagna"). The geography of Piedmont is 43.3% mountainous, along with extensive areas of hills (30.3%) and plains (26.4%).

Piedmont is the second largest of [Italy's 20 regions](/wiki/Regions_of_Italy "Regions of Italy"), after [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily"). It is broadly coincident with the upper part of the [drainage basin](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin") of the river [Po](/wiki/Po_River "Po River"), which rises from the slopes of Monviso in the west of the region and is Italy's largest river. The Po drains the semicircle formed by the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps") and [Apennines](/wiki/Apennines "Apennines"), which surround the region on three sides.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Lago-Maggiore_1387.JPG/250px-Lago-Maggiore_1387.JPG)](/wiki/File:Lago-Maggiore_1387.JPG)

A view over Piedmont's [Lake Maggiore](/wiki/Lake_Maggiore "Lake Maggiore") in [Stresa](/wiki/Stresa "Stresa") with the [Borromean Islands](/wiki/Borromean_Islands "Borromean Islands")

The countryside is very diverse: from the rugged peaks of the massifs of Monte Rosa and Gran Paradiso to the damp rice paddies of Vercelli and Novara, from the gentle hillsides of the [Langhe](/wiki/Langhe "Langhe"), [Roero](/wiki/Roero "Roero"), and [Montferrat](/wiki/Montferrat "Montferrat") to the plains. 7.6% of the entire territory is considered [protected area](/wiki/Protected_area "Protected area"). There are 56 different national or regional parks; one of the most famous is the [Gran Paradiso National Park](/wiki/Gran_Paradiso_National_Park "Gran Paradiso National Park"), between Piedmont and the [Aosta Valley](/wiki/Aosta_Valley "Aosta Valley").

Piedmont has a typically [temperate climate](/wiki/Temperate_climate "Temperate climate"), which on the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps") becomes progressively temperate-cold and colder as it climbs to altitude. In areas located at low altitudes, winters are relatively cold but not very rainy and often sunny, with the possibility of snowfall, sometimes abundant. Snowfall, on the other hand, is less frequent and occasional in the northeast areas. Summers are hot with local possibilities of strong thunderstorms.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

As of 2025, Piedmont has a population of 4,251,868, of which 48.9% are male and 51.1% are female, compared to the national average of 49.0% and 51.0% respectively. Minors make up 14.0% of the population, and seniors make up 26.9%, compared to the national average of 14.9% and 24.7% respectively.[[2]](#cite_note-population-2)

The population density in Piedmont is lower than the national average, at 167.5 inhabitants per km2 compared to the national figure of 195.1. The [Metropolitan City of Turin](/wiki/Metropolitan_City_of_Turin "Metropolitan City of Turin") has 322.9 inhabitants per km2, whereas [Verbano-Cusio-Ossola](/wiki/Verbano-Cusio-Ossola "Verbano-Cusio-Ossola") is the least densely populated province, with only 67.8 inhabitants per km2.[[2]](#cite_note-population-2)[[1]](#cite_note-area-1)

The population of Piedmont followed a downward trend throughout the 1980s, a result of the natural negative balance (of some 3 to 4% per year), while the migratory balance since 1986 has again become positive because of immigration.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Piedmont&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

Main article: [Politics of Piedmont](/wiki/Politics_of_Piedmont "Politics of Piedmont")

The Regional Government (*Giunta Regionale*) is presided by the president of the region (*presidente della regione*), who is elected for a five-year term and is composed of the president and 14 ministers, including a vice president (*vice presidente*).[[17]](#cite_note-18) In the [2010 Piedmontese regional election](/wiki/2010_Piedmontese_regional_election "2010 Piedmontese regional election"), which took place on 29–30 March, [Roberto Cota](/wiki/Roberto_Cota "Roberto Cota") of [Lega Nord](/wiki/Lega_Nord "Lega Nord") defeated incumbent [Mercedes Bresso](/wiki/Mercedes_Bresso "Mercedes Bresso") of the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(Italy) "Democratic Party (Italy)") (PD). For the [2014 Piedmontese regional election](/wiki/2014_Piedmontese_regional_election "2014 Piedmontese regional election"), Cota chose not to stand again for president and the parties composing his coalition failed to agree on a single candidate, resulting in a landslide victory for [Sergio Chiamparino](/wiki/Sergio_Chiamparino "Sergio Chiamparino"), a member of the PD who had been [mayor of Turin](/wiki/Mayor_of_Turin "Mayor of Turin") from 2001 to 2011. Chiamparino was in charge until the [2019 Piedmontese regional election](/wiki/2019_Piedmontese_regional_election "2019 Piedmontese regional election"), when [Alberto Cirio](/wiki/Alberto_Cirio "Alberto Cirio") of [Forza Italia](/wiki/Forza_Italia_(2013) "Forza Italia (2013)") became the new president of the region.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Piedmont&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Economy")]

The [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") (GDP) of the region was 137.4 billion euros in 2018, accounting for 7.8% of Italy's GDP. [GDP per capita](/wiki/GDP_per_capita "GDP per capita") at [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") was 31,300 euros or 104% of the EU27 average in the same year. The GDP per employee was 111% of the EU average.[[18]](#cite_note-19) Since 2006, the [Piemonte Agency for Investments, Export and Tourism](/wiki/Piemonte_Agency_for_Investments,_Export_and_Tourism "Piemonte Agency for Investments, Export and Tourism") began to facilitate outside investment and promote Piedmont's industry and tourism. It was the first Italian institution to combine the activities being carried out by pre-existing local organizations to promote the territory internationally.

### Automotive

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Piedmont&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Automotive")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Fiat-500-vorne2.jpg/250px-Fiat-500-vorne2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fiat-500-vorne2.jpg)

[Fiat 500e](/wiki/Fiat_500e "Fiat 500e")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Maserati_Levante_Allegro_Antinori_One_of_One_Genf_2019_1Y7A5992.jpg/250px-Maserati_Levante_Allegro_Antinori_One_of_One_Genf_2019_1Y7A5992.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maserati_Levante_Allegro_Antinori_One_of_One_Genf_2019_1Y7A5992.jpg)

[Maserati Levante](/wiki/Maserati_Levante "Maserati Levante")

The region contains major industrial centres, the most important of which is Turin, home to the [Fiat](/wiki/Fiat "Fiat") conglomerate, but mass-market Fiat cars are not produced anymore, only small-scale manufacturing of luxury [Maserati](/wiki/Maserati "Maserati") cars (36,702 in 2020).[[19]](#cite_note-carprod-20) Most of the ex-Fiat plants now belong to other companies: aerospace is owned by [Leonardo S.p.A.](/wiki/Leonardo_S.p.A. "Leonardo S.p.A."), turbo jet engines by [General Electric](/wiki/General_Electric "General Electric"), high-speed trains by [Alstom](/wiki/Alstom "Alstom"), bearings by [SKF](/wiki/SKF "SKF"). Fiat no longer exists as an independent company; car production belongs to [Stellantis](/wiki/Stellantis "Stellantis"), and trucks, buses, tractors, agriculture and construction machines are produced by the independent company [CNH Industrial](/wiki/CNH_Industrial "CNH Industrial") (most manufacturing activity takes place in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), in Piedmont only the production of [New Holland](/wiki/New_Holland_Construction "New Holland Construction") excavators in [San Mauro Torinese](/wiki/San_Mauro_Torinese "San Mauro Torinese") and [Iveco](/wiki/Iveco#Engines "Iveco") diesel engines in [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin")). Neither of them are headquartered in [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin") anymore, however, some research and development centres are still working.

Formerly famous automotive design companies also were sold to global automotive groups: [Italdesign Giugiaro](/wiki/Italdesign_Giugiaro "Italdesign Giugiaro") to [Volkswagen](/wiki/Volkswagen "Volkswagen"), [Ghia](/wiki/Ghia "Ghia") to [Ford](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Company "Ford Motor Company"), [Pininfarina](/wiki/Pininfarina "Pininfarina") to [Mahindra](/wiki/Mahindra_%26_Mahindra "Mahindra & Mahindra"); [Bertone](/wiki/Gruppo_Bertone "Gruppo Bertone") went into bankruptcy in 2014. The massive decline in the automotive industry caused other regions like [Veneto](/wiki/Veneto "Veneto") (€163 billion in 2018) and [Emilia-Romagna](/wiki/Emilia-Romagna "Emilia-Romagna") (€161 billion in 2018) to surpass Piedmont (€137 billion in 2018) in GDP and led to relative high unemployment. The peak of [Italian motor vehicle production](/wiki/Automotive_industry_in_Italy#Production_figures "Automotive industry in Italy") is reached in 1989 with 2.22 million units, but in 2019 (before the [COVID-19 pandemic in Italy](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Italy "COVID-19 pandemic in Italy")) it was only 0.92 million units. Even existing Italian car production now relocated to [Southern Italy](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy"), such as in [Pomigliano d'Arco](/wiki/Alfa_Romeo_Pomigliano_d%27Arco_plant "Alfa Romeo Pomigliano d'Arco plant") (140,478 in 2020), [Melfi](/wiki/Melfi "Melfi") (229,848 in 2020), and [Atessa](/wiki/Atessa "Atessa") (257,026 in 2020), because of cost cutting.[[19]](#cite_note-carprod-20)

There are some automotive suppliers of:

* exhaust systems, electronic systems, suspension systems and automotive lighting in [Venaria Reale](/wiki/Venaria_Reale "Venaria Reale") and [Rivalta di Torino](/wiki/Rivalta_di_Torino "Rivalta di Torino") from [Magneti Marelli](/wiki/Magneti_Marelli "Magneti Marelli")
* [dual-clutch transmission](/wiki/Dual-clutch_transmission "Dual-clutch transmission"), gearboxes, drivelines and their mechatronics components from [Dana Graziano](/wiki/Graziano_Trasmissioni "Graziano Trasmissioni")
* bearings from [SKF](/wiki/SKF "SKF")
* tires ([Michelin](/wiki/Michelin "Michelin") and [Pirelli](/wiki/Pirelli "Pirelli"))

### Electronics and industrial equipment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Piedmont&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Electronics and industrial equipment")]

There are some important companies in high-tech manufacturing: [Comau](/wiki/Comau "Comau") (industrial robots) and Prima Industrie (laser equipment). Silicon wafer production is in [Novara](/wiki/Novara "Novara") by MEMC. [Olivetti](/wiki/Olivetti "Olivetti"), once a major [electronics industry](/wiki/Electronics_industry "Electronics industry") whose plants were in [Scarmagno](/wiki/Scarmagno "Scarmagno") and [Ivrea](/wiki/Ivrea "Ivrea"), has now turned into a small-scale computer service company and no longer produces computers. [Leonardo](/wiki/Leonardo_S.p.A. "Leonardo S.p.A.") Elettronica in Turin-Caselle develops and manufactures airborne mission systems and airborne computers. Machine building has a long tradition in Piedmont with the manufacturing of excavators, telescopic handlers, industrial refrigerators, printing machines, paper machines, packaging machines, glass machines, turbines, and high-speed trains.

### Agriculture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Piedmont&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Agriculture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/The_rice_fields_near_Vercelli.jpg/250px-The_rice_fields_near_Vercelli.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_rice_fields_near_Vercelli.jpg)

Rice fields between [Novara](/wiki/Novara "Novara") and [Vercelli](/wiki/Vercelli "Vercelli")

Lowland Piedmont is a fertile agricultural region. The main agricultural products in Piedmont are [cereals](/wiki/Cereals "Cereals"), including [rice](/wiki/Rice "Rice"), representing more than 10% of national production, [maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize"), [grapes](/wiki/Grape "Grape") for [wine](/wiki/Wine "Wine")-making, [fruit](/wiki/Fruit "Fruit") and [milk](/wiki/Milk "Milk").[[22]](#cite_note-europa1-23) With more than 800,000 head of cattle in 2000, livestock production accounts for half of total agricultural production in Piedmont.

[Piedmont](/wiki/Piemonte_(wine) "Piemonte (wine)") is one of the great winegrowing regions in Italy. More than half of its 700 km2 (170,000 acres) of vineyards are registered with [DOC](/wiki/Denominazione_di_Origine_Controllata "Denominazione di Origine Controllata") designations. It produces prestigious wines as [Barolo](/wiki/Barolo "Barolo") and [Barbaresco](/wiki/Barbaresco "Barbaresco") from the [Langhe](/wiki/Langhe "Langhe") near [Alba](/wiki/Alba_(CN) "Alba (CN)"), and the [Moscato d'Asti](/wiki/Moscato_d%27Asti "Moscato d'Asti") and sparkling [Asti](/wiki/Asti_(wine) "Asti (wine)") from the vineyards around [Asti](/wiki/Asti "Asti"). The city of Asti is about 55 km (34 mi) east of Turin in the plain of the Tanaro River and is one of the most important centres of [Montferrat](/wiki/Montferrat "Montferrat"), one of the best known Italian wine districts in the world, declared officially on 22 June 2014 a UNESCO World Heritage site.[[23]](#cite_note-24) Indigenous grape varieties include [Nebbiolo](/wiki/Nebbiolo "Nebbiolo"), [Barbera](/wiki/Barbera "Barbera"), [Dolcetto](/wiki/Dolcetto "Dolcetto"), [Freisa](/wiki/Freisa "Freisa"), [Grignolino](/wiki/Grignolino "Grignolino") and [Brachetto](/wiki/Brachetto "Brachetto").

Tourism in Piedmont employs 75,534 people and involves 17,367 companies operating in the hospitality and catering sector, with 1,473 hotels and other tourist accommodation. The sector generates a turnover of €2,671 million, 3.3% of the €80,196 million total estimated spending on tourism in Italy. The region is popular with both foreign visitors and those from other parts of Italy. In 2002 there were 2,651,068 total arrivals, 1,124,696 (42%) of whom were foreign. The traditional leading areas for tourism in Piedmont are the Lake District ("Piedmont's riviera"), which accounts for 32.84% of total overnight stays, and the metropolitan area of Turin, which accounts for 26.51%.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

In 2006, Turin hosted the [XX Olympic Winter Games](/wiki/XX_Olympic_Winter_Games "XX Olympic Winter Games"), and in 2007 it hosted the [XXIII Universiade](/wiki/2007_Winter_Universiade "2007 Winter Universiade"). Alpine tourism tends to concentrate in a few highly developed stations like [Alagna Valsesia](/wiki/Alagna_Valsesia "Alagna Valsesia") and [Sestriere](/wiki/Sestriere "Sestriere"). Around 1980, the long-distance trail [Grande Traversata delle Alpi](/wiki/Grande_Traversata_delle_Alpi "Grande Traversata delle Alpi") (GTA) was created to draw more attention to the variety of remote, sparsely inhabited valleys. Within the tourism industry in Piedmont, a reference to the system of Royal Residences has to be made. First of all, it is part of the UNESCO World Heritage Sites since 1997 and, secondly, it represents a peculiarity of the region, since such a network cannot be found elsewhere in Italy. The Residences of the Royal House of Savoy belong to the historical and cultural heritage of Piedmont and nowadays they play a central role in the tourism field.[[25]](#cite_note-26) In a reality in which the tourism industry is characterized by an amalgam of several players and stakeholders, the creation of a system or network like that of the Royal Residences represents an added benefit for the whole territory as well as a competitive edge.[[26]](#cite_note-27) Therefore, considering that tourism is a key factor in the creation of long-lasting value and working in a cooperative and collaborative perspective is essential,[[27]](#cite_note-28) the network of the Royal Residences represents an example worth of notice.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Piedmont&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Transport")]

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Piedmont&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Air")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Airbus_A320_of_Lufthansa_with_sharklets_%28D-AIZS%29.jpg/250px-Airbus_A320_of_Lufthansa_with_sharklets_%28D-AIZS%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Airbus_A320_of_Lufthansa_with_sharklets_(D-AIZS).jpg)

[Turin-Caselle International Airport](/wiki/Turin_International_Airport "Turin International Airport")

[Turin-Caselle International Airport](/wiki/Turin_International_Airport "Turin International Airport") has domestic and international flights and handled 3,952,158 passengers and 3,334 tons of cargo in 2019 (before the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic")).[[22]](#cite_note-europa1-23)

### Land

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Piedmont&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Land")]

There are links with neighbouring France via the [Fréjus](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9jus_Road_Tunnel "Fréjus Road Tunnel") and [Colle di Tenda](/wiki/Colle_di_Tenda "Colle di Tenda") tunnels as well as the [Montgenèvre Pass](/wiki/Col_de_Montgen%C3%A8vre "Col de Montgenèvre"). Piedmont also connects with [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") by the [Simplon](/wiki/Simplon_Pass "Simplon Pass") and [Great St Bernard](/wiki/Great_St_Bernard "Great St Bernard") passes. It is possible to reach Switzerland via a normal road that crosses eastern Piedmont, starting from [Arona](/wiki/Arona,_Piedmont "Arona, Piedmont") and ending in [Locarno](/wiki/Locarno "Locarno"), on the [Swiss border](/wiki/Italy%E2%80%93Switzerland_border "Italy–Switzerland border"). The region has the longest motorway network amongst the Italian regions, covering approximately 800 km (500 mi). It radiates from Turin, connecting it with the other provinces in the region, as well as with the other regions in Italy. In 2001, the number of passenger cars per 1,000 inhabitants was 623 (above the national average of 575).[[22]](#cite_note-europa1-23) There is a [Turin–Milan high-speed railway](/wiki/Turin%E2%80%93Milan_high-speed_railway "Turin–Milan high-speed railway"); the travel time is only 52 minutes.

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Piedmont&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Culture")]

### Languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Piedmont&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Languages")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Piedmontese_Language_distribution.png/250px-Piedmontese_Language_distribution.png)](/wiki/File:Piedmontese_Language_distribution.png)

[Piedmontese language](/wiki/Piedmontese_language "Piedmontese language") distribution:

Areas where Piedmontese is spoken (municipalities where [Occitan](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language") and [Arpitan](/wiki/Franco-Proven%C3%A7al "Franco-Provençal") presence is only de jure are included)

Areas where Piedmontese is spoken alongside other languages (Occitan, Arpitan and [Alemannic](/wiki/Alemannic_German "Alemannic German")) and areas of linguistic transition (with [Ligurian](/wiki/Ligurian_language "Ligurian language") and with [Lombard](/wiki/Lombard_language "Lombard language"))

As in the rest of Italy, [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") is the official national language. The main local languages are [Piedmontese](/wiki/Piedmontese_language "Piedmontese language"), [Insubric](/wiki/Western_Lombard_dialect "Western Lombard dialect") (spoken in the eastern part of the region), [Occitan](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language") (spoken by a minority in the [Occitan Valleys](/wiki/Occitan_Valleys "Occitan Valleys") situated in the [Province of Cuneo](/wiki/Province_of_Cuneo "Province of Cuneo") and the [Metropolitan City of Turin](/wiki/Metropolitan_City_of_Turin "Metropolitan City of Turin")), and [Franco-Provençal](/wiki/Franco-Proven%C3%A7al_language "Franco-Provençal language") (spoken by another minority in the alpine heights of the Metropolitan City of Turin), like in the [Susa Valley](/wiki/Susa_Valley "Susa Valley") and [Walser](/wiki/Walser_language "Walser language") (spoken by a minority in the [Province of Vercelli](/wiki/Province_of_Vercelli "Province of Vercelli") and [Province of Verbano-Cusio-Ossola](/wiki/Province_of_Verbano-Cusio-Ossola "Province of Verbano-Cusio-Ossola")).

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Piedmont&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

Main article: [Piedmontese cuisine](/wiki/Piedmontese_cuisine "Piedmontese cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/LovageAgnoCattailPurslane_%288312537016%29.jpg/250px-LovageAgnoCattailPurslane_%288312537016%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:LovageAgnoCattailPurslane_(8312537016).jpg)

*[Vitello tonnato](/wiki/Vitello_tonnato "Vitello tonnato")*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Polenta_e_bruscitti_%282%29.jpg/250px-Polenta_e_bruscitti_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Polenta_e_bruscitti_(2).jpg)

*[Bruscitti](/wiki/Bruscitti "Bruscitti")* served with *[polenta](/wiki/Polenta "Polenta")* porridge

[Piedmontese cuisine](/wiki/Piedmontese_cuisine "Piedmontese cuisine") is the style of cooking in the [Northern Italian](/wiki/Northern_Italy "Northern Italy") region of Piedmont. Bordering [France](/wiki/France "France") and [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"), Piedmontese cuisine is partly influenced by [French cuisine](/wiki/French_cuisine "French cuisine"); this is demonstrated in particular by the importance of appetizers, a set of courses that precede what is traditionally called a first course and aimed at whetting the appetite. In France these courses are fewer and are called *entrée*.[[34]](#cite_note-PI-35)

It is a region in Italy with the largest number of [cheeses](/wiki/Category:Piedmontese_cheeses "Category:Piedmontese cheeses") and [wines](/wiki/Piedmont_wine "Piedmont wine"). The most prestigious Italian culinary school, the [University of Gastronomic Sciences](/wiki/University_of_Gastronomic_Sciences "University of Gastronomic Sciences"), was founded in Piedmont. Similar to other Northern Italian cuisines, veal, wine, and butter are among the main ingredients used in cooking.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

Some well-known dishes include *[agnolotti](/wiki/Agnolotti "Agnolotti")*, *[vitello tonnato](/wiki/Vitello_tonnato "Vitello tonnato")* (also popular in Argentina), and *[bagna càuda](/wiki/Bagna_c%C3%A0uda "Bagna càuda")*. Piedmont is also credited for the famous pasta dish [tagliolini](/wiki/Tagliolini "Tagliolini") (*tajarin* in [Piedmontese](/wiki/Piedmontese_language "Piedmontese language")).[[36]](#cite_note-37) *Tagliolini* are a type of egg [pasta](/wiki/Pasta "Pasta") normally made fresh by hand. According to Italian writer and journalist Massimo Alberini, *tagliolini* was among [King Victor Emmanuel II](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_II "Victor Emmanuel II")'s preferred dishes.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

Common in [Verbano-Cusio-Ossola](/wiki/Province_of_Verbano-Cusio-Ossola "Province of Verbano-Cusio-Ossola") area[[38]](#cite_note-caffeinamagazine-39) are *[bruscitti](/wiki/Bruscitti "Bruscitti")*, originating from [Alto Milanese](/wiki/Alto_Milanese "Alto Milanese"), a dish of braised meat cut very thin and cooked in wine and [fennel seeds](/wiki/Fennel "Fennel"), historically obtained by stripping leftover meat.

The [Slow Food Movement](/wiki/Slow_Food "Slow Food") was started in Piedmont by [Carlo Petrini](/wiki/Carlo_Petrini "Carlo Petrini") who was from the town of [Bra, Piedmont](/wiki/Bra,_Piedmont "Bra, Piedmont"). The movement greatly benefited the region by highlighting Piedmont's diverse cuisine. The Slow Food Movement offices are still headquartered in the town of Bra.

The town of [Alba](/wiki/Alba,_Piedmont "Alba, Piedmont") is known for its gourmet food. It is also the region where [Alba white truffles](/wiki/Tuber_magnatum "Tuber magnatum") are found.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

Since 2006, the Piedmont region has benefited from the start of the [Slow Food](/wiki/Slow_Food "Slow Food") movement and [Terra Madre](/wiki/Terra_Madre "Terra Madre"), events that highlighted the rich agricultural and [viticultural](/wiki/Viticulture "Viticulture") value of the Po Valley and northern Italy. A chain of food halls [Eataly](/wiki/Eataly "Eataly") works in collaboration with [Slow Food](/wiki/Slow_Food "Slow Food"). Piedmont is the leading producer of confectionery, coffee, rice, and white truffles in Italy. It is ranked 3 of 20 for the production of quality DOC and DOCG wines with 1,982,718 hl, there are 17 DOCG wines of all possible types (white, red, sweet, sparkling). In 2019, Piedmont accounted for 16.5% of wine exports from Italy, ranking second behind [Veneto](/wiki/Veneto "Veneto"), with 36%.[[40]](#cite_note-41) The typical food industries in Piedmont are:

* alcoholic beverages
  + production of quality dry red wines from [Nebbiolo](/wiki/Nebbiolo "Nebbiolo"), [Dolcetto](/wiki/Dolcetto "Dolcetto"), and [Barbera](/wiki/Barbera "Barbera") grapes
  + production of quality dry white wines
  + production of sweet white wines from [Erbaluce](/wiki/Erbaluce "Erbaluce") grapes
  + production of [vermouth](/wiki/Vermouth "Vermouth"), which was invented in Piedmont
  + production of sparkling wine [Asti Spumante](/wiki/Asti_wine "Asti wine"), [Alta Langa](/wiki/Alta_Langa "Alta Langa"), [Gavi](/wiki/Cortese_di_Gavi "Cortese di Gavi")
* coffee
  + production of coffee [Lavazza](/wiki/Lavazza "Lavazza")
  + production of coffee/chocolate liqueur [Bicerin](/wiki/Bicerin "Bicerin")
* confectionery
  + production of traditional [Chocolate Gianduiotto](/wiki/Gianduiotto "Gianduiotto")
  + production of different kind of [chocolate](/wiki/Chocolate "Chocolate") and [pralines](/wiki/Praline_(nut_confection) "Praline (nut confection)")
  + production of [torrone](/wiki/Torrone "Torrone")
  + production of sweet paste [Nutella](/wiki/Nutella "Nutella")
  + production of [biscotto](/wiki/Biscuit "Biscuit")
* delicacy
  + production of [white truffles](/wiki/Tuber_magnatum "Tuber magnatum") from [Alba](/wiki/Alba,_Piedmont "Alba, Piedmont") and related products with white truffles like condiments, honey, salami, and prosciutto
  + production of high-quality marinated beef Gradisca or dried beef [Bresaola](/wiki/Bresaola "Bresaola")
* cereals
  + production of dry [risotto](/wiki/Risotto "Risotto") mixes

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Piedmont&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Sport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Derby_di_Torino_-_Serie_A_1976-1977.jpg/250px-Derby_di_Torino_-_Serie_A_1976-1977.jpg)](/wiki/File:Derby_di_Torino_-_Serie_A_1976-1977.jpg)

[Derby della Mole](/wiki/Derby_della_Mole "Derby della Mole") between [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus_FC "Juventus FC") and [Torino](/wiki/Torino_FC "Torino FC") on 3 April 1977

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/2006_Olympics_Opening_Ceremony.jpg/250px-2006_Olympics_Opening_Ceremony.jpg)](/wiki/File:2006_Olympics_Opening_Ceremony.jpg)

[2006 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics") opening ceremony in Turin

In [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), notable clubs in Piedmont include [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin")-based [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus "Juventus") and [Torino](/wiki/Torino_F.C. "Torino F.C."), who have won 43 official top-flight league championships (as of the [2020–21 season](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Serie_A "2020–21 Serie A")) between them (36 titles won by Juventus and seven by Torino), more than any other city in Italy. Juventus is the most successful club in Italy, having won the most league titles (36), [Coppa Italia](/wiki/Coppa_Italia "Coppa Italia") titles (14), and [Supercoppa Italiana](/wiki/Supercoppa_Italiana "Supercoppa Italiana") titles (9) of any team in the country; [Juventus Women](/wiki/Juventus_Women "Juventus Women"), established in 2017, also achieved success, immediately becoming one of the country's most successful women's teams. Other smaller teams include the old "Piedmont Quadrilateral" components [Novara](/wiki/Novara_Calcio "Novara Calcio"), [Alessandria](/wiki/U.S._Alessandria_Calcio_1912 "U.S. Alessandria Calcio 1912"), [Casale](/wiki/Associazione_Sportiva_Casale_Calcio "Associazione Sportiva Casale Calcio"), and [Pro Vercelli](/wiki/Pro_Vercelli "Pro Vercelli"). With the pre-[World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") success of Pro Vercelli in 1910s and Juventus in 1930s, as well as winning cycles of Torino during the *[Grande Torino](/wiki/Grande_Torino "Grande Torino")* years and Juventus in different eras since 1950, the region became the most successful in terms of championships won. Casale and [Novese](/wiki/U.S.D._Novese "U.S.D. Novese") contributed with one *[scudetto](/wiki/Scudetto "Scudetto")* each. Other local teams include volleyball teams [Cuneo](/wiki/Piemonte_Volley "Piemonte Volley") (male) and [AGIL Novara](/wiki/AGIL_Volley "AGIL Volley") (female), basketball teams Biella Basketball and Junior Casale, ice hockey team Hockey Club Turin, and roller hockey side [Amatori Vercelli](/w/index.php?title=Amatori_Vercelli&action=edit&redlink=1 "Amatori Vercelli (page does not exist)"), who have won three league titles, an Italian Cup, and two [CERS Cups](/wiki/CERS_Cup "CERS Cup").

Turin hosted the [2006 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics").[[41]](#cite_note-42) The 2006 Winter Olympics ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *2006 Olimpiadi invernali*), officially the XX Olympic Winter Games ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *XX Giochi olimpici invernali*) and also known as Torino 2006, were a winter [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") held from 10 to 26 February in [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin"), Italy. This marked the second time Italy had hosted the [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games"), the first being in [1956](/wiki/1956_Winter_Olympics "1956 Winter Olympics") in [Cortina d'Ampezzo](/wiki/Cortina_d%27Ampezzo "Cortina d'Ampezzo"); Italy had also hosted the [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games") in [1960](/wiki/1960_Summer_Olympics "1960 Summer Olympics") in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"). Turin was selected as the host city for the 2006 Games in June 1999. The official motto of Torino 2006 was "Passion lives here".[[42]](#cite_note-Motto-43) The Games' logo depicted a stylized profile of the [Mole Antonelliana](/wiki/Mole_Antonelliana "Mole Antonelliana") building, drawn in white and blue ice crystals, signifying the snow and the sky. The crystal web was also meant to portray the web of new technologies and the Olympic spirit of community. The 2006 Olympic mascots were Neve ("snow" in Italian), a female [snowball](/wiki/Snowball "Snowball"), and Gliz, a male [ice cube](/wiki/Ice_cube "Ice cube").[[43]](#cite_note-Mascots-44)
